dsls.markdown:
# Overview of CS Discoveries #### Welcome to Module 3! This module will provide you with an overview of the CS Discoveries course with an emphasis on concepts students will learn and tools students will use throughout the course. Engaging in this module will help you to make decisions about how to select the course content that you will implement in your classroom. ### **You will:** * #### Gain a high-level understanding of the CS Discoveries curriculum. * #### Become familiar with the student learning tools used within CS Discoveries. * #### Decide which units of CS Discoveries you will teach in your class. #### You will begin with a lesson focused on the content of the CS Discoveries course.
#### **Suggested time:** 60 minutes

# Course Overview ## Computer Science Discoveries is: * #### An introductory computer science course for 6 - 10th grade students. * #### Mapped to CSTA standards. * #### Focused on topics such as problem solving, programming, physical computing, user centered design, and data. * #### Designed to offer students opportunities to build their own websites, apps, animations, games, physical computing systems and data models.

https://studio.code.org/s/self-paced-pl-.long_instructions:
## Consider the following when watching this video:

#### What aspects about CS Discoveries will be most exciting for my students?

<div>

dsls.markdown:
# Units #### CS Discoveries is divided into seven units. Units are structured to give students context and motivation for the content before skill building. Units begin with a lesson that leverages students' knowledge and experience in problem solving and technology. This lays the foundation for the skills and content that the unit introduces.

## Click on the units below to learn about the course content:
💡 Unit 1: Problem Solving and Computing

#### The **Problem Solving and Computing** unit is a highly interactive and collaborative introduction to the field of computer science, as framed within the broader pursuit of solving problems. Through a series of puzzles, challenges, and real world scenarios, students are introduced to a problem solving process that they will return to repeatedly throughout the course. Students then learn how computers input, output, store, and process information to help humans solve problems within the context of apps. The unit concludes with students designing an app that helps solve a problem of their choosing.

💻 Unit 2: Web Development

#### In **Web Development**, students are empowered to create and share content on their own web pages. They begin by thinking about the role of the web and how it can be used as a medium for creative expression. As students develop their pages and begin to see themselves as programmers, they are encouraged to think critically about the impact of sharing information online and how to be more critical consumers of content. They are also introduced to problem solving as it relates to programming while they learn valuable skills such as debugging, using resources, and teamwork. The unit has a large focus on designing web pages for users' needs. At the conclusion of the unit, students will have created a personal website they can publish and share.

🖱️ Unit 3: Interactive Animations and Games

#### In the **Interactive Animations and Games** unit, students build on their coding experience as they create programmatic images, animations, interactive art, and games. Starting off with simple, primitive shapes and building up to more sophisticated sprite-based games, students become familiar with the programming concepts and the design process computer scientists use daily. They then learn how these simpler constructs can be combined to create more complex programs. In the final project, students develop a personalized, interactive program. Along the way, they practice design, testing, and iteration, as they come to see that failure and debugging are an expected and valuable part of the programming process.

🧑🏾‍🎨 Unit 4: The Design Process

#### The **Design Process** unit transitions students from thinking about computer science as a tool to solve their own problems towards considering the broader social impacts of computing. Through a series of design challenges, students are asked to consider and understand the needs of others while developing a solution to a problem. The second half of the unit consists of an iterative team project, during which students have the opportunity to identify a need that they care about, prototype solutions both on paper and in App Lab, and test their solutions with real users to get feedback and drive further iteration.

📊 Unit 5: Data and Society

#### The **Data and Society** unit is about the importance of using data to solve problems and it highlights how computers can help in this process. The first chapter explores different systems used to represent information in a computer and the challenges and tradeoffs posed by using them. In the second chapter, students learn how collections of data are used to solve problems, and how computers help to automate the steps of this process. In the final project, students gather their own data and use it to develop an automated solution to a problem.

👩🏽‍💻 Unit 6: Creating Apps with Devices

#### In the **Physical Computing** unit, students explore the role of physical devices in computing. Using App Lab and **either** Adafruit's Circuit Playground **or** the BBC micro:bit, students develop programs that utilize the same hardware inputs and outputs that you see in the smart devices, looking at how a simple rough prototype can lead to a finished product. Then, students explore how physical devices can be used to react to the world around them using a “maker” mindset to create prototypes with everyday materials.
#### _**Note:** Unit 6 has **two** options - Option A with the Circuit Playground and Option B with the micro:bit. Teachers should **not** complete both options as they are the same basic lessons._

🧠 Unit 7: AI and Machine Learning

#### In the **AI and Machine Learning** unit, students learn how computers can find patterns in data to make decisions. Students use the Problem Solving Process for machine learning to define a problem, prepare their data, train a model, then test and evaluate their model for accuracy and potential bias. Students explore a variety of scenarios and datasets that lend themselves to machine learning. They also explore some of the modern problems with machine learning, especially around bias and impact.


## Want to learn more? #### Each unit of CS Discoveries has a corresponding self-paced professional learning module that takes a deeper dive into unit content. To explore those modules, scroll down to the "Middle school teachers" section of our self-paced professional learning page.

# Chapters #### Units are divided into two chapters. Chapters often include several small projects and activities that provide an opportunity for students to practice new skills. If you are short on time, you may choose to complete only the first chapter of a unit. ## Click on the units below to learn about the big questions addressed in each chapter:
💡 Unit 1: Problem Solving and Computing

### **Chapter 1 - The Problem Solving Process** * #### What strategies and processes can I use to become a more effective problem solver? ### **Chapter 2 - Computers and Problem Solving** * #### How do computers help people to solve problems? * #### How do people and computers approach problems differently? * #### What does a computer need from people in order to solve problems?

💻 Unit 2: Web Development

### **Chapter 1 - Creating Web Pages** * #### How do web developers create web pages for users? * #### How can text communicate a web page's content, structure, and style? * #### How do I safely and appropriately create content for the Internet? * #### What strategies can I use when coding to find and fix issues? ### **Chapter 2 - Multi-Page Websites** * #### How can websites be used to address problems in the world? * #### What strategies can teams use to work better together? * #### How do I know what information can be trusted online?

🖱️ Unit 3: Interactive Animations and Games

### **Chapter 1 - Images and Animations** * #### What is a computer program? * #### What are the core features of most programming languages? * #### How does programming enable creativity and individual expression? * #### What practices and strategies will help me as I write programs? ### **Chapter 2 - Building Games** * #### How do software developers manage complexity and scale? * #### How can programs be organized so that common problems only need to be solved once? * #### How can I build on previous solutions to create even more complex behavior?

🧑🏾‍🎨 Unit 4: The Design Process

### **Chapter 1: User Centered Design** * #### How do computer scientists identify the needs of their users? * #### How can we ensure that a user's needs are met by our designs? * #### What processes will best allow us to efficiently create, test, and iterate upon our design? ### **Chapter 2: App Prototyping** * #### How do teams effectively work together to develop software? * #### What roles beyond programming are necessary to design and develop software? * #### How do designers incorporate feedback into multiple iterations of a product?

📊 Unit 5: Data and Society

### **Chapter 1: Representing Information** * #### Why is representation important in problem solving? * #### What features does a representation system need to be useful? * #### What is necessary to create usable binary representation systems? * #### How can we combine systems together to get more complex information? ### **Chapter 2: Solving Data Problems** * #### How does data help us to solve problems? * #### How do computers and humans use data differently? * #### What parts of the data problem solving process can be automated? * #### What kinds of real world problems do computers solve by using data?

👩🏽‍💻 Unit 6: Creating Apps with Devices

### **Chapter 1: Inputs and Outputs** * #### What inputs and outputs are available on a physical device? * #### What inputs and outputs are available on an app? * #### How can we create apps that use a physical device to control a digital app? ### **Chapter 2: Building Physical Prototypes** * #### How can a physical device use sensors to react to a physical environment? * #### How can simple hardware be used to develop innovative new products?

🧠 Unit 7: AI and Machine Learning

### **Chapter 1 - Understanding Machine Learning** * #### How does machine learning find patterns in data to make decisions? * #### How can we avoid bias when training a machine learning model? ### **Chapter 2 - Design a Machine Learning App** * #### How can machine learning be used to solve problems in our community?

https://studio.code.org/s/self-paced-pl-.long_instructions:
## Consider the following when watching this video:

#### What excites you most about teaching CS Discoveries?

dsls.markdown:
# Lessons #### Each chapter of CS Discoveries consists of individual lessons that share common characteristics. Knowing the types of lessons in the curriculum will help you understand the intent and purpose along with your role in teaching the lesson. ## Lesson Length #### Most CS Discoveries lessons are designed for 45 minute class periods, but can be modified for longer or shorter class periods. Some lessons, such as project lessons, take multiple days to complete. Suggested times are included in each lesson plan.

## Lesson Components
🙋‍♀️ Warm Up

#### Each lesson begins with a warm up, which serves as a highly approachable shared experience that highlights the need for the skills and understandings presented in the lesson. These should be kept short so that students can quickly move into the main activity of the lesson. Warm ups end with a big question that articulates the problem space students will be exploring during the lesson. This question sets the stage for the target learning.

💃 Activity

#### Students spend the majority of their time engaging in the lesson activity. Activities provide students with opportunities to explore new computing concepts. Exploration is essential for students to build the content knowledge they will need to understand and participate in computing activities. Lesson activities are scaffolded to provide students with just-in-time information to solve their problems as they build their computing knowledge and skills.

🏁 Wrap Up

#### Wrap ups end every lesson and provide students with opportunities to reflect on the skills and understandings presented in the lesson. Wrap ups include a review of any vocabulary introduced in the lesson, and return to the big question introduced in the warm up. Wrap ups are often journal prompts and short discussions that can be used as an exit ticket or short formative assessment.

## Lesson Types
💻 Programming Lessons

#### Programming lessons introduce new programming concepts and skills to students. Although students will spend a majority of their time on the computer during these lessons, programming lessons also include offline components. Students are expected to complete many of the activities in small groups or as a class. The teacher plays an active role facilitating opportunities for sense making and formative assessment as students engage in lesson content.

✍🏽 Non-programming Lessons

#### Non-Programming lessons focus on process skills and non-programming computing concepts. These lessons are mostly offline, but some do include online components. Offline lessons typically include an activity guide that students will use throughout the lesson as a guide for their learning. Non-programming lessons present students with opportunities for collaboration and discussion in small groups as well as individual reflection.

👩‍💻 Project Lessons

#### ***Mini-projects*** are opportunities for students to apply what they have learned by creating something new in a more open-ended setting. Mini-projects appear throughout the unit as opportunities for students to practice open-ended application of a small set of new skills. These projects can be expanded or condensed depending on the class time available. #### ***Projects*** are multi-day open-ended lessons found at the end of each chapter or unit. Projects challenge students to create using skills developed throughout the unit. Projects often include opportunities for planning, creation, and reflection.

# Task: Compare and Contrast Lesson Types #### Use the links below to preview a lesson plan for each lesson type. Pay attention to similarities and differences between the lessons. * ### Programming Lesson * ###Non-Programming Lesson * ### Project Lesson

# Levels #### When students are on the computer, activities look like a series of bubbles, called levels, that students complete in order. Read the information below to learn more about the different types of levels found in the CS Discoveries curriculum. ## Level Icons #### When looking at lesson plans and course resources, you will notice several different icons representing different types of levels within a given lesson.
#### These levels feature Code.org tools, including online tools and environments for creating projects. An instructions panel appears on these levels to explain any new content introduced in the level and provide a list of tasks to complete. Teachers can review their students' code.
#### All Code.org courses feature videos for students designed to introduce tools or to help explain new concepts. Students can watch the videos together as a class or individually on their computers.
#### Choice levels contain multiple activities for students to choose from. Some choice levels are practice levels intended to provide students with multiple opportunities to practice skills learned in previous levels. Some choice levels are challenge levels intended to allow students and opportunity to explore new concepts not covered in the lesson objectives.
#### Text levels contain instructions, text, or images to help run a class activity. Lesson instructions will indicate how these levels should be incorporated into the activity.
## Purpose of Levels in the Curriculum #### Alongside a level's icon are labels that indicate the purpose of that level within the lesson. These labels can help you develop expectations for students or think about your role as the teacher.
#### **Predict:** These levels present students with a completed program and asked to predict what the code will do. After typing in a response, students can run the code and see the result. These are best completed in pairs or as whole-class discussion.
#### **Skill building:** These levels are usually targeted tasks that are focused on specific skill building rather than open ended creation. At the beginning of a lesson, these tasks are highly scaffolded, and this scaffolding falls away as students develop the target skills.
#### **Assessment:** Assessment levels are levels that can be used to assess student mastery of the target skill of the lesson. These levels are marked with a purple bubble and a small check mark. Some assessment levels include multiple choice or matching levels that are auto graded. Teachers are able to view and provide feedback on programming assessment levels.
#### **Challenge:** These levels present students with more open-ended tasks to complete as an extension of the skills they've learned in the lesson. Any new skills or coding commands introduced in these levels are not required for future lessons, but are offered as enrichment and additional practice.
#### **Project:** _(not pictured)_ In these levels, students build more extensive programs using skills learned throughout the unit. Project levels are often accompanied by an activity guide that is intended to help students plan their project and reflect on their process.
#### Click here to learn more about teaching a programming lesson.

SP-VPL-21-mod3-CSD U3 Variables Predict.bubble_choice_description: Predict levels prompt students to actively engage with working code before coding for themselves.

callouts.grid: Click here to show the grid.

contained levels.long_instructions:
# Predict Levels

Predict levels allow a student to examine code and write in a prediction before running a program. Predict levels are typically followed by a class discussion where students can explain how they made their prediction, and talk through whether or not the program's behavior matched their prediction.

# Do this:

* Type in a prediction into the input box below, then press Run

callouts.grid: Click here to show the grid.

SP-VPL-21-mod3-CSD U3 Variables Draw Pop.bubble_choice_description: Programming levels are usually targeted tasks that are focused on specific skill-building rather than open-ended creation.
SP-VPL-21-mod3-CSD U3 Variables Draw Pop.long_instructions:
# Programming Levels

Programming Levels allow students to write code to solve problems. Instructions for each level are included at the top, while the center of the screen is their workspace for writing code. Students can run their code on the left-side of the screen. Additional resources are provided in the Help and Tips tab, and an example solution is provided in the blue teacher-only menu on the right side of the screen.

# Do This

* Explore the **Toolbox** area of the workspace. Notice that only commands relevant to this level are available to students.
* Click on the **Help and Tips** Tab to see additional resources
* Open the Teacher menu and click the blue Example Solution button to view the solution to this level
* When you are finished, click the orange finish button that will appear under the preview screen after you run the code.

SP-VPL-21-mod3-CSD U3 Variables assessme.bubble_choice_description: Assessment levels are levels that can be used to assess student mastery of the target skill of the lesson.
SP-VPL-21-mod3-CSD U3 Variables assessme.short_instructions: Change the program to use a variable for the size of the two eyes, and run it a few times with different values assigned to the variable, as in the sample image.
SP-VPL-21-mod3-CSD U3 Variables assessme.long_instructions: # Assessment Levels These levels can be used for formative assessment during a lesson and to provide feedback to students. There is an additional **Rubric** Tab that provides guidance for how well students complete the level. As a teacher, you can also leave feedback in this tab that students can check. Assessment levels typically appear with a purple checkmark in the curriculum. # Do This * Click on the **Rubric** Tab to view the mini-rubric for this level * When you are finished, click the orange finish button that will appear under the preview screen after you run the code.

mini_rubric.rubric_key_concept: Use variables to store and update information
mini_rubric.rubric_performance_level_1: The eyeSize variable is used such that the two eyes have the same size as defined by eyeSize, with no other unexpected changes. The program is free of syntax errors.
mini_rubric.rubric_performance_level_2: The eyeSize variable is used such that the two eyes have the same size as defined by eyeSize. Other changes to the code may have introduced other unexpected behaviors, or there may be minor syntax errors that do not prevent the code from running.
mini_rubric.rubric_performance_level_3: The eyeSize variable has been used somewhere in the program, but not such that it runs as instructed.
mini_rubric.rubric_performance_level_4: There are no changes to the code, or the changes appear unrelated to variables, or variables are used in a way that prevents the program from running.

SP-VPL-21-mod3-CSD U2 text style text al.bubble_choice_description: Challenge levels expand on content presented in the lesson and offer additional opportunities for open-ended creation.
SP-VPL-21-mod3-CSD U2 text style text al.long_instructions:
# Challenge Levels

These levels are at the end of a the main programming levels, and include additional challenges or new code to stretch and enrich student work. Challenge levels never include core content for a unit and students do not need to master challenge levels to be successful in the curriculum.

# Do This

* Click on the "Help and Tips" tab above to see the resources students are given to help them learn more about the new concept or skill in the challenge - in this case, the `font-family` property in the "Text Properties" Guide.
* When you are finished, click the orange finish button in the bottom right hand corner of your screen.
SP-VPL-21-mod3-CSD U2 text style text al.teacher_markdown:
## Note on fonts and font families

For a web browser to display a font, the font **must** be available on the device the web browser is running on.  There's no guarantee that any device has a particular font, so it's much safer to use font **families**, which allow for many different fonts that have the same general look at feel.

If students want to specify an exact font, they'll need to use a font from the web, so the browser can download that specific font to use when rendering the page.  More information on these fonts can be found at [Google Fonts](https://fonts.google.com/) and [W3Schools](https://www.w3schools.com/Css/css3_fonts.asp).

SP-VPL-21-mod3-CSDU4 Project Bug Feature.bubble_choice_description: In project levels, students build more extensive programs using skills learned throughout the unit.
SP-VPL-21-mod3-CSDU4 Project Bug Feature.long_instructions:
![expandable](https://images.code.org/7087c26d323b584954530dc545a36e7a-image-1628716001505.gif)
Click to expand
# Project Levels Projects can span multiple levels, so project levels let students carry their code from one level to another. You can identify a project level by the interlocked circles icon at the top of the Workspace. The final project level lets students submit their project, including a timestamp of when the project was completed. Click the image on the right to see an example of what this looks like. Project levels also tend to allow students to share and remix their projects using the buttons in the upper-left corner. Remixing a project creates a copy of their project that they can continue to work on. # Do This * Hover over the interlocking circles icon in the **Workspace** to learn more about what linked project levels mean * Click the image in the upper-right of the instructions to see how the submit interface looks for students * Click the "Share" button in the upper-left corner to see how students can share their work with others. * When you are finished, click the orange "Finish" button that will appear under the preview screen after you run the code.

dsls.markdown: # The Problem Solving Process #### **The Problem Solving Process** is a tool for structured problem solving that is woven throughout the entire CS Discoveries course to promote student growth and development. The Problem Solving Process is first introduced to students in Unit 1 and thereafter, students use a version of the Problem-Solving Process that is specific to the unit content. ## 🔎 Define * #### Determine the problem you are trying to solve * #### Identify your constraints * #### Describe what success will look like ## 📝 Prepare * #### Brainstorm/research possible solutions * #### Compare pros and cons * #### Make a plan ## 🖥️ Try * #### Put your plan into action ## 🤔 Reflect * #### Compare your results to the goals you set while defining the problem * #### Decide what you can learn from this or do better next time * #### Identify any new problems you have discovered

https://studio.code.org/s/self-paced-pl-.long_instructions:
## Consider the following when watching this video:

#### How might your students benefit from using the problem solving process to solve problems?

dsls.markdown:
# Problem Solving Process #### Having a strategy for approaching problems can help students develop new insights and come up with new and better solutions. This is an iterative process that is broadly useful for solving all kinds of problems. ## Problem Solving Across The Curriculum #### The Problem Solving Process is applied differently across different units. The following bullet points demonstrate how the process shifts throughout the CSD course.
* #### **The Problem Solving Process** is used in Unit 1: Problem Solving and Computing and is the template for each of the other problem solving processes used throughout the course.
* #### **The Problem Solving Process with Programming** is used in mini-projects and chapter projects of Unit 2: Web Development, Unit 3: Animations and Games, and Unit 6: Creating Apps with Devices.
* #### **The Problem Solving Process with Design** is primarily used in Unit 4: The Design Process. #### _This version of the Problem Solving Process does appear in the first lesson of Unit 2: Web Development, Unit 3: Animations and Games, and Unit 6: Creating Apps with Devices._
* #### **The Problem Solving Process with Data** is used in Unit 5: Data and Society and Unit 7: AI and Machine Learning.

# Overview of Learning Tools 

#### Now that we have a high level understanding of the course content, we are going to take a closer look at the learning tools that students will engage with throughout the course. 

### **You will:**

* #### Become familiar with the student learning tools used within CS Discoveries.

# Tools Across The Course #### CS Discoveries introduces students to **tools and programming languages that are accessible for beginners** while offering more advanced students opportunities to create sophisticated projects. All of the tools below are integrated directly into the Code.org website, allowing teachers to have visibility into all student work and progress.
## Web Lab #### Web Lab is a browser-based tool for creating and publishing HTML and CSS web sites. ### **Units that use Web Lab:** #### Web Development
## Game Lab #### Game Lab is a browser-based JavaScript programming environment designed to create sprite-based drawings, animations, and games. Students are able to switch between programming in blocks or text. ### **Units that use Game Lab:** #### Interactive Animations and Games
## App Lab #### App Lab is a browser-based JavaScript programming environment for creating interactive apps. Students are able to switch between programming in blocks or text. ### **Units that use App Lab:** #### The Design Process #### Creating Apps with Devices - Option A: Circuit Playground #### Creating Apps with Devices - Option B: micro:bit #### AI and Machine Learning ##### _**Note:** Teachers should **not** teach both Creating Apps with Devices options as they are the same basic lessons. Instead, teachers should decide which version of this unit they wish to teach based on the device they would like to use._
## AI Lab #### AI Lab is a browser-based tool for creating machine learning models from tabular data. ### **Units that use AI Lab:** #### AI and Machine Learning

# Welcome to Game Lab!

##### Game Lab is the programming environment that students will use in Unit 3: Interactive Animations and Games as they learn basic programming concepts and build their own animations and games. As students program their animations and games using either blocks or text, they will be able to test their code using the preview section on the left hand side of the screen.

# Do this:

* ##### Click the orange button to **try a sample game.** In this game, use the up arrow to make the frog jump.  The frog must avoid the mushrooms while trying to get the flies.  Every fly scores one point, and hitting the mushrooms makes the player health go down.  When your health hits zero, the game is over.
* ##### When you are finished playing the game, click the orange finish button that will appear under the preview screen after you run the code.

# Welcome to App Lab!

##### App Lab is the programming environment that students will use to develop apps in Unit 4: The Design process and Unit 7: AI & Machine Learning. 

# Do This

* ##### Click on the orange run button below the app preview screen to **try a sample app.**
* ##### When you are finished exploring the sample app, click the orange finish button that will appear under the preview screen after you run the code.

dsls.markdown:
# App Lab + Circuit Playground or micro:bit #### Both the Circuit Playground and the micro:bit use a set of additional commands available in App Lab. Using the same drag-and-drop editor that students have become comfortable with in App Lab and Game Lab, students can turn on LEDs, read sensors, and write programs that use physical hardware for user input and output.
#### **Circuit Playground** #### **micro:bit**

🆚 Circuit Playground vs micro:bit

#### The **Circuit Playground** is a small microcontroller board with LEDs, buttons, and sensors built in. It is designed specifically for novice programmers, enabling students to get up and running quickly with physical computing without worrying about many of the traditional barriers to entry. #### The **micro:bit** is a pocket-sized computer with an LED light display, buttons, sensors, and many input/output features that, when programmed, let students interact with the device and the world. The many built in features of the micro:bit allow beginners to do interesting things immediately without wiring.

🔌 How do these devices connect to App Lab?

## WebSerial #### The Circuit Playground and micro:bit can connect directly to computers while using Google Chrome or Microsoft Edge browsers. WebSerial is the recommended way to connect to your board. ### **Set Up** #### 1. Connect the board to your computer. #### 2. Press the “Connect to Board” button. #### 3. A window should pop up with a row that includes "Circuit Playground" or "micro:bit". Select that row, then click "Connect". #### 4. You should see several green checkmarks below. When full connected, the Circuit Playground will display a sequence of green lights and emit a series of beeps, and the micro:bit will show a short animation. #### That's it! Just those **four** easy steps! If you have any issues, please refer to our Support Article.

❓ Do I need a class set of the Circuit Playground or the micro:bit?

#### You **will** need a class set of the Circuit Playgrounds to teach Unit 6 - Option A OR a class set of the micro:bits to each Unit 6 - Option B of CS Discoveries. #### If you do not have access to either Circuit Playgrounds or micro:bits, you will be able to teach all units of CS Discoveries except Unit 6: Creating Apps with Devices.
#### Click here to learn more about the **Circuit Playground** and how to purchase Circuit Playgrounds for your classroom. #### Click here to learn more about the **micro:bit** and how to purchase micro:bits for your classroom.

# AI Lab #### AI Lab is an environment that lets students interact with tabular data to train a machine learning model.
## How does AI Lab work? #### Students can select from several pre-generated datasets, or upload their own data. They then decide what they would like to predict and which features to base their prediction off of. AI Lab will analyze the data and create a machine learning model which students can import into App Lab. Before saving their machine learning model, students also need to save a Model Card which acts like the documentation for their model. ## Do students learn how to code in AI Lab? #### No, students don't do any coding in AI Lab itself. Instead, students can import their machine learning models into App Lab to create machine learning apps.

dsls.content1: Match the statement with the appropriate tool.

questions.text: I am looking for a tool that my students can use to make web pages using HTML and CSS.
I would like to use a tool where students can learn fundamental programming concepts while being given the freedom to create their own games using either text or blocks.
My students are interested in designing apps that they can share with their friends.
I want to teach my students how to use tabular data to train a machine learning model.

dsls.title: Check for Understanding

dsls.markdown:
# Overview of Implementation

#### CS Discoveries can be flexibly taught as a single semester, two semesters over multiple years, or as a full-year course. Options are even available for less than one semester.  As you begin planning for your CS class, the first step is to decide which content you will include in your course. 

### **You will:**

* #### Decide which units of CS Discoveries you will teach in your class.

# Tech Requirements and Required Materials ## Technical Requirements #### **This course requires each student to have access to their own internet-connected computer for each CS Discoveries lesson, whether in a classroom or computer lab setting.** Each computer must have a modern web browser installed. All of the course tools and resources (lesson plans, teacher dashboard, videos, student tools, programming environment, etc.) are online and accessible through a web browser. For more information about tech setup go to: code.org/educate/it #### **It is NOT required that students have access to internet-connected computers at home.** ## Suggested Materials / Supplies ### **General Course Materials:** * #### **Printed handouts:** Many lessons have handouts that are designed to guide students through activities. While it is not required that all of these handouts be printed, many were designed to be printed and we highly recommend printing when possible. * #### **Classroom supplies:** Beyond printing, some lessons call for typical classroom supplies and manipulatives such as: * #### Student journals * #### Poster paper * #### Markers/colored pencils * #### Scissors * #### Scrap paper * #### Glue or tape * #### Post-it notes (or similar sized scrap paper) * #### Rulers or a straight edge of some kind * #### Index cards (or similar sized scrap paper) ___ ### **Unit-Specific Materials:** #### In addition to those general course materials, the following items are called for in specific units: ### **Unit 1 - Problem Solving and Computing** * #### Lesson 1 - Introduction to Problem Solving (Aluminum Boats): * #### Aluminum foil * #### Container for water * #### Pennies *(pennies can be replaced with some other kind of weight of the same size.* ##### _**Note:** Alternate activities are available if you do not have access to these supplies. See the dropdown section below to view the alternate options._
Alternate Lesson 1 Activities

#### Lesson 1 has alternate lesson activities available if you do not have access to the supplies listed above. These alternate lessons include:
* #### **Lesson 1: Alternate Lesson Option 1** - Introduction to Problem Solving (Newspaper Table): * #### A set of newspapers * #### Several tape dispensers
* #### **Lesson 1: Alternate Lesson Option 2** - Introduction to Problem Solving (Spaghetti Bridge): * #### Several boxes of spaghetti * #### Several glue guns
* #### **Lesson 1: Alternate Lesson Option 3** - Introduction to Problem Solving (Paper Tower): * #### A ream of printer paper


### **Unit 6 - Creating Apps with Devices (Option A and Option B)** * #### **Option A**: Classroom set of Circuit Playgrounds. Check out code.org/maker/circuitplayground for more details. * #### **Option B**: Classroom set of micro:bit devices. Check out code.org/maker/microbit for more details. * #### Maker supplies, such as empty tissue boxes or cardboard rolls or scratch paper ##### _**Note:** The Creating Apps with Devices unit has **two** implementation options - one for those using circuit playgrounds and one for those using micro:bit. Teachers should **not** buy both sets of devices or teach both unit options as they are equivalent and cover the same content._

# Implementation Options

CS Discoveries can be taught as a full-year course, a single semester course, or multiple courses split across grade levels. We recommend always starting with Chapter 1 of Unit 1 to introduce the problem solving process and classroom norms. If students have already completed this content in another grade level, you may use one of the alternate lessons available for introducing the problem solving process.

#### Consider the following when selecting the content you will teach in your CS Discoveries class.
## What are the unit dependencies? #### Some units in CS Discoveries rely on concepts that are taught in previous units. Every unit relies on the Problem Solving Process, which is thoroughly introduced in Chapter 1 of the first unit and reintroduced in the first lesson of chapter 1 for most units (_Unit 5 reintroduces the Problem Solving Process in the first lesson of chapter 2_). Unit dependencies are highlighted on the CS Discoveries course overview page and in the CS Discoveries Implementation Guide. ## Which standards will I cover? #### CS Discoveries is mapped to the CSTA standards. Click here to view the list of standards addressed in each unit. ## How much instructional time do I have? #### As you plan your course pacing, use the unit calender at the top of each Unit Overview page to adjust for your instructional minutes. If you are short on time and want to cover more topics with your students, consider teaching the first chapter of multiple units.

#### For more information, click here to read the full CS Discoveries Implementation Guide.

#### As the teacher, you play an active role in customizing the learning experience for your students.

## Use the space below to document your plan for implementing CS Discoveries. ### **Consider the following when making your plan:** * #### Which units will you include in your course? * #### Which unit will you start with? * #### Will you teach full units, or one chapter from multiple units? * #### When will you teach each unit that you have selected? * #### Will you teach different units across grade levels? If yes, which content will you include in each grade level course?
dsls.title: Make a Plan: Implementation Planning
